studyMetadata.title: Jude for Today
studyMetadata.subtitle: Bible Study
studyMetadata.author: Rick Creighton
studyMetadata.description:
Not every message about God is trustworthy. How do we recognise truth from error? What should we do when faith is distorted or misused? Jude writes with urgency to address that very situation.
‘I felt compelled to write and urge you to contend for the faith that was once for all entrusted to God’s holy people.’ (Jude 1:3)
His words are direct and serious. The truth about God is not something to reshape or ignore. It has been given, and it must be guarded. Jude calls his readers not just to believe, but to stand firm—holding on to what is true even when it is challenged.
studyMetadata.shareMessage:
Check out this Bible study app — “Bible Study Reader”.

Available soon on Google Play and the App Store.

theme.theme-annotation: Jude study theme — muted bronze and slate palette. Serious and weighty without harshness, reflecting themes of warning, discernment, and contending for the faith once delivered.

elements.text: Introduction

The letter of Jude is short but urgent. Written to believers facing false teaching, it calls Christians to stand firm in the truth. Jude reminds us that the gospel is not something to move beyond — it is something to defend and hold onto.

As we begin, we’ll see both the security believers have in God and the responsibility they have to contend for the faith.
Read Jude 1–2
Jude begins not by focusing on problems, but on God’s work in his people.

elements.question: Who does Jude say the letter is written to?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jude writes to those who are ’called,‘ ‘loved in God the Father,‘ and ’kept for Jesus Christ.’ The letter is addressed to believers — people who have been chosen by God, held in his love, and preserved by him for Christ.
elements.questionHint: Look at the very first verse — what three describing words or phrases does Jude use to identify the people he is writing to?

elements.question: What three things are said about believers in verse 1?
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are described as called, loved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ. All three are passive — things done to believers by God rather than things they have done themselves. They are called by God’s initiative, loved within the Father’s own love, and kept secure by his power.
elements.questionHint: Each of the three descriptions is something being done to believers rather than something they are doing — what does each word tell you about who is acting, and who is being acted upon?

elements.question: What does verse 2 say God multiplies to his people?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 2 is a blessing in which Jude wishes that mercy, peace, and love would be multiplied to his readers. These are not things they earn but gifts God lavishes on his people — and the word ’multiplied’ suggests abundance rather than a bare minimum.
elements.questionHint: Verse 2 is a short wish or blessing from Jude — what three gifts does he wish to be increased in the lives of his readers?

elements.text: Even before addressing challenges, Jude reminds believers of their identity. We are secure because of God’s calling, love, and preserving power.
Read Jude 3
Jude had originally intended to write a different kind of letter — but something urgent changed his plans.

elements.question: What did Jude originally want to write about?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jude says he was eager to write about ’our common salvation’ — the shared gospel experience that unites all believers. His original intention was a positive, encouraging letter about the salvation they all shared in Christ.
elements.questionHint: Jude says he was ’eager’ to write about a particular topic before circumstances changed — what was that topic, and how does it contrast with what he ends up writing about?

elements.question: What does he now urge believers to do?
elements.sampleAnswer: Instead of writing about salvation generally, Jude urges his readers to ’contend earnestly for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.’ The change in plan reflects the urgency of the situation — the faith is under threat and needs to be defended.
elements.questionHint: Jude uses a strong word — ‘earnestly’ — to describe the kind of effort he is calling for. What is the specific action he urges, and what is it directed toward protecting?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’contend for the faith’?
elements.sampleAnswer: The word ’contend’ carries the sense of striving or struggling — it is a word used of athletic or military effort. To contend for the faith means to defend and hold firmly to the gospel truth that has been handed down, not allowing it to be distorted or abandoned. It involves both knowing the truth and being willing to stand for it when it is challenged.
elements.questionHint: The word ’contend’ suggests something more active than simply believing — what kind of effort or posture does the word imply, and what might it require of someone in practice?

elements.text: The ’faith’ here refers to the message of the gospel — the truth about Jesus that has been handed down once for all. It is not changing or evolving; it is something to be preserved and defended.
Read Jude 4
Jude now explains why this call to contend is necessary.

elements.question: What problem has arisen among the believers?
elements.sampleAnswer: Certain people have crept in unnoticed among the believers — false teachers who were marked out for condemnation long ago. The problem is not just external opposition but internal infiltration: dangerous teaching has entered the community from within, making it harder to identify and resist.
elements.questionHint: Jude uses the word ’crept in’ — what does that phrase tell you about how these people came to be among the believers, and why that makes them particularly difficult to deal with?

elements.question: What do these people do with God’s grace?
elements.sampleAnswer: These people pervert or turn God’s grace into a licence for sensuality — using the truth of God’s forgiveness as an excuse to live immorally. Rather than grace leading to holy living, they twist it into permission to indulge sinful desires.
elements.questionHint: Jude says they ’turn’ grace into something — what direction does he say they turn it, and how does that contrast with what grace is actually meant to produce in a believer’s life?

elements.question: What do they deny about Jesus Christ?
elements.sampleAnswer: They deny Jesus Christ as ’our only Master and Lord.’ Their false teaching amounts to a rejection of his authority and his exclusive claim over their lives, even while they may have used the language of Christianity.
elements.questionHint: Jude gives Jesus two titles in verse 4 — what are they, and what would it mean to deny both of them in the way these false teachers do?

elements.text: False teaching often distorts grace — turning it into permission to sin rather than a reason to live for God. Jude warns that this is not a small issue; it strikes at the heart of the gospel.
Conclusion

Believers are secure in God’s love and keeping — but that security does not lead to passivity. It leads to action. We are called to hold firmly to the truth and to guard it carefully.

The Christian life is not just about believing the gospel — it is also about contending for it when it is challenged or distorted.
Questions for Reflection

elements.question: Why is it important to remember your identity in Christ before confronting error?
What might ’contending for the faith’ look like in your context?
How can grace be misunderstood or misused today?

elements.text: Introduction
Jude now strengthens his warning by pointing to examples from history. These examples show that God takes sin and false teaching seriously — and that judgment is real.
Read Jude 5–7
Jude reminds his readers of well-known examples.

elements.question: What happened to those who were saved out of Egypt but did not believe?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jude reminds his readers that the Lord saved the people of Israel out of Egypt, but afterwards destroyed those who did not believe. The point is stark: being a recipient of God’s saving action does not guarantee final safety if that is followed by persistent unbelief and rebellion.
elements.questionHint: Jude says the Lord did two things — first he saved the people, then he destroyed some of them. What was the difference between those who were saved initially and those who were subsequently destroyed?

elements.question: What happened to the angels who did not stay in their proper position?
elements.sampleAnswer: The angels who did not keep their proper position of authority but abandoned their dwelling place have been kept by God in eternal chains under darkness, reserved for the judgment of the great day. Their abandonment of assigned boundaries resulted in permanent imprisonment awaiting final judgment.
elements.questionHint: Jude describes both what the angels did and what happened to them as a consequence — what was the specific action they took, and what did God do in response?

elements.question: What example do Sodom and Gomorrah provide?
elements.sampleAnswer: Sodom and Gomorrah, along with the surrounding cities, committed sexual immorality and pursued unnatural desire, and they serve as an example by undergoing the punishment of eternal fire. They are held up as a visible, historical warning of what God’s judgment looks like when sin is not turned from.
elements.questionHint: Jude says Sodom and Gomorrah ’serve as an example’ — an example of what, specifically, and for whom?

elements.text: These examples show a consistent pattern: privilege does not guarantee safety if there is rebellion and unbelief.
Read Jude 8–10
Jude now describes the behavior of the false teachers.

elements.question: What three things characterize these people in verse 8?
elements.sampleAnswer: In verse 8, Jude says these people defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme the glorious ones. Despite having witnessed or heard the same warnings as others, they persist in moral corruption, rebellion against God-given authority, and contemptuous speech against angelic beings.
elements.questionHint: Verse 8 gives three separate characteristics — one relating to the body, one relating to authority, and one relating to speech. Can you identify which phrase in the verse corresponds to each of those three areas?

elements.question: How does Jude contrast them with Michael the archangel?
elements.sampleAnswer: Even Michael the archangel, when disputing with the devil over the body of Moses, did not dare to pronounce a blasphemous judgment but instead said ’The Lord rebuke you.’ The contrast is pointed: the most powerful of angels showed restraint and deferred to God’s authority, whereas these false teachers boldly blaspheme things they do not even understand.
elements.questionHint: Michael is a far more powerful being than these false teachers — yet he showed restraint in a dispute with the devil. What does that comparison tell you about the arrogance Jude is highlighting in their behaviour?

elements.question: What does verse 10 say about their understanding?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 10 says they blaspheme all that they do not understand. What they do understand — things known by instinct like unreasoning animals — they use to destroy themselves. Their confident speaking is built on ignorance, and even the natural understanding they do possess leads them toward ruin rather than away from it.
elements.questionHint: Jude makes a two-part observation about their understanding: there are things they speak about but don’t understand, and things they do understand but that harm them. What does that combination tell you about the state of their knowledge?

elements.text: False teachers are marked by arrogance and a rejection of authority. They speak confidently about things they do not truly understand.
Read Jude 11–13
Jude uses vivid imagery and Old Testament examples.

elements.question: Which three figures are mentioned in verse 11, and what do they represent?
elements.sampleAnswer: The three figures are Cain, Balaam, and Korah. Cain represents murderous hatred driven by jealousy and unbelief. Balaam represents false teaching motivated by financial gain — the willingness to lead people astray for profit. Korah represents rebellion against God’s appointed authority. Together they form a portrait of the false teachers’ character: corrupt in motive, destructive in effect, and rebellious in spirit.
elements.questionHint: Each of the three Old Testament figures is associated with a particular kind of failure — what specific sin or pattern does each one represent, and how might each apply to the false teachers Jude is describing?

elements.question: What images does Jude use to describe these people?
elements.sampleAnswer: Jude uses five vivid images: hidden reefs at love feasts (dangerous and concealed, causing shipwreck); waterless clouds blown along by winds (promising but delivering nothing); fruitless trees in late autumn, twice dead and uprooted (lifeless and beyond recovery); wild waves of the sea casting up foam of their own shame (destructive and shameful); and wandering stars for whom the gloom of utter darkness has been reserved (directionless and heading toward permanent darkness).
elements.questionHint: Jude uses five separate images from nature — look at each one and ask what quality of the false teachers each image is designed to capture: their danger, their emptiness, their fruitlessness, their shamelessness, or their ultimate destiny?

elements.text: These descriptions highlight their danger: they are not just mistaken — they are destructive, especially within the community of believers.
Read Jude 14–16
Jude now speaks of coming judgment.

elements.question: What does Enoch’s prophecy say the Lord will do?
elements.sampleAnswer: Enoch’s prophecy says the Lord will come with ten thousands of his holy ones to execute judgment on all and to convict all the ungodly of all their deeds of ungodliness, and of all the harsh things ungodly sinners have spoken against him. The judgment is comprehensive — it covers ungodly deeds and ungodly words, and no one escapes its reach.
elements.questionHint: Enoch’s prophecy describes both the scale of the Lord’s coming and the scope of what will be judged — what two categories of sin does the prophecy say will be brought into judgment?

elements.question: What are these people characterized by in verse 16?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verse 16 characterises them as grumblers and malcontents who follow their own sinful desires, who speak loudly — with boastful speech — and who flatter people to gain advantage. The portrait is of people driven by self-interest, who use words both to complain about what they don’t have and to manipulate others into giving it to them.
elements.questionHint: Verse 16 describes four characteristics — two relating to how they speak negatively, one relating to what drives their behaviour, and one relating to how they use flattery. Can you identify each of those four in the verse?

elements.text: God’s judgment is not uncertain or distant — it is assured. False teaching and ungodly living will ultimately be exposed and judged.
Conclusion

Jude’s warnings are strong because the danger is real. False teaching leads people away from Christ and toward judgment.

These verses remind us that God’s grace does not mean indifference to sin. Instead, it calls us to take truth seriously and to recognize the consequences of rejecting it.
Questions for Reflection

elements.question: What do these examples teach us about God’s attitude toward sin?
Why is false teaching so dangerous within the church?
How can we recognize similar patterns today?

elements.text: Introduction
After strong warnings, Jude turns to encouragement and instruction. Believers are not left helpless — they are given clear direction for how to live faithfully in the midst of challenges.
Read Jude 17–19
Jude reminds believers of what they have already been told.

elements.question: What had the apostles warned about?
elements.sampleAnswer: The apostles of Jesus Christ had warned that in the last time there would be scoffers following their own ungodly passions. The appearance of such people is therefore not a surprise or a sign that something has gone wrong — it is exactly what God’s messengers predicted would happen.
elements.questionHint: Jude says believers should ’remember’ what the apostles predicted — what specifically did they say would happen ’in the last time,‘ and why does remembering this matter for how believers should respond to it?

elements.question: What characterizes these scoffers?
elements.sampleAnswer: They follow their own ungodly passions, cause divisions, are worldly people, and do not have the Spirit. Despite perhaps being present in the Christian community, they are spiritually empty — their behaviour is self-driven rather than Spirit-led, and their effect on the community is divisive rather than unifying.
elements.questionHint: Verses 18–19 give several characteristics of these scoffers — look at what they follow, what they cause, and what they lack. What is the most significant thing verse 19 says about their inner spiritual condition?

elements.text: The presence of false teachers should not surprise believers. It is something God has already prepared his people for.
Read Jude 20–21
Now Jude gives practical instructions.

elements.question: What four things are believers told to do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Build yourselves up in your most holy faith • Pray in the Holy Spirit • Keep yourselves in God’s love • Wait for the mercy of Jesus Christ
elements.sampleAnswer: Believers are told to: build themselves up in their most holy faith; pray in the Holy Spirit; keep themselves in the love of God; and wait for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ that leads to eternal life. Taken together, these form a picture of an active, Trinitarian, forward-looking life of faith — each person of the Trinity is present, and the believer is engaged rather than passive.
elements.questionHint: Each of the four instructions in verses 20–21 involves a different dimension of the Christian life — look at them together and notice that all three persons of the Trinity are mentioned. Which instruction corresponds to each person, and what does the fourth instruction point toward?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’keep yourselves in God’s love’?
elements.sampleAnswer: To keep yourself in God’s love does not mean earning or maintaining God’s affection by performance — his love for his people is not conditional on their behaviour. Rather, it means remaining in the sphere of his love: living in a way that responds to it, not drifting from it through neglect or disobedience. It is the posture of someone who stays close to God rather than wandering away, keeping themselves oriented toward him and his purposes.
elements.questionHint: God’s love for his people is not something they can earn or lose — so if that’s the case, what do you think Jude means by instructing believers to ’keep themselves’ in it? What kind of action might that require?

elements.text: Believers do not earn God’s love — but they are called to remain in it, living in response to it and not drifting away.
Read Jude 22–23
Jude now addresses how to relate to others.

elements.question: How should believers treat those who doubt?
elements.sampleAnswer: Those who doubt are to be treated with mercy. Jude doesn’t call for harsh judgment or dismissal of those who are wavering — he calls for a compassionate, patient response. Doubt is not the same as deliberate rebellion, and those in it need to be met with gentleness rather than condemnation.
elements.questionHint: Jude distinguishes between different people who need to be helped in different ways — what specific quality does he say should characterize the response to those who are doubting, and why might that be particularly appropriate for doubt rather than for defiant sin?

elements.question: What does it mean to ’snatch others from the fire’?
elements.sampleAnswer: To snatch others from the fire is to intervene urgently and decisively to rescue someone who is in immediate spiritual danger — someone heading toward judgment whose situation requires more than gentle encouragement. The image implies emergency: you don’t politely discuss options with someone whose clothing is on fire. The rescue requires speed, directness, and a willingness to act even if it is uncomfortable.
elements.questionHint: The image of snatching from fire suggests a situation that doesn’t allow time for lengthy deliberation — what does the urgency of the image tell you about the spiritual state of the people being described, and what kind of response it requires?

elements.question: What balance is needed when showing mercy?
elements.sampleAnswer: Mercy is to be shown with fear — hating even the garment stained by the flesh. Jude calls for a combination of compassionate engagement and careful self-protection. You go close enough to help, but not so close that you are drawn into the same error or sin. The balance is between genuine mercy toward the person and a firm revulsion toward the sin that has corrupted them — caring for the person without becoming contaminated by what has affected them.
elements.questionHint: Jude says to show mercy ’with fear’ — these two things might seem to pull in opposite directions. What do you think the fear is directed at, and why is it important to hold both mercy and fear together rather than choosing just one?

elements.text: There is both compassion and caution. We are called to help others without being drawn into the same errors ourselves.
Read Jude 24–25
Jude ends with a powerful reminder of God’s ability.

elements.question: What is God able to do for believers?
elements.sampleAnswer: God is able to keep believers from stumbling and to present them blameless before his glorious presence with great joy. The entire letter has described the threat of false teaching and the difficulty of remaining faithful — and this doxology responds by anchoring the believer’s confidence not in their own ability to stand, but in God’s ability to keep them. He is both willing and able to bring them safely to the end.
elements.questionHint: Verse 24 describes two things God is able to do — one in the present journey and one at the final destination. What are they, and how do they address the concern that a believer might not make it through the dangers Jude has described?

elements.question: How does this give confidence in the Christian life?
elements.sampleAnswer: The confidence it gives is not confidence in ourselves but in God. Throughout Jude’s letter the dangers have been real and the responsibility of believers has been clear — but the final word is not about human effort. It is about divine power. God is able — and the word ’able’ carries the sense of more than sufficient capacity. The believer’s ultimate security rests not on how well they contend, but on the one who keeps them. This transforms the call to faithfulness from a burden into a response to grace.
elements.questionHint: Jude has spent much of the letter calling believers to active effort — contending, building, praying, keeping. How does ending with ’God is able to keep you’ change the foundation on which all that effort rests?

elements.text: The letter ends where it began — with God. Our perseverance ultimately depends not on our strength, but on his.
Conclusion

Jude’s message is both sobering and encouraging. Believers are called to be alert, to grow in faith, and to care for others — but above all, to trust in God’s power to keep them.

The Christian life involves effort and responsibility, but it is grounded in God’s preserving grace.
Questions for Reflection

elements.question: Which of the instructions in verses 20–21 do you most need to grow in?
How can you help others who are struggling with doubt or error?
How does God’s promise to keep you shape your confidence and daily life?

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Jude
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A three-session Bible study on the book of Jude.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: Christians who want to think more carefully
blocks.body: Jude was written to people who already believed — but who were under pressure from teaching that twisted the faith they had received. If you are a Christian who senses that something matters about getting doctrine right, but are not quite sure how to think it through, this study is for you.

blocks.title: People exploring what Christianity actually claims
blocks.body: If you are not yet a Christian but are genuinely curious — wondering not just what Christians believe, but why they think it matters so much — Jude is a striking place to start. It is a letter written with urgency, by someone who believed the truth about God was worth standing up for. That raises its own questions worth sitting with.

blocks.title: Leaders and group facilitators
blocks.body: Jude deals directly with the challenge of false teaching within a community of faith — which makes it particularly relevant for anyone in a leadership or pastoral role. If you are guiding others through questions of doctrine, discernment, or how to handle people who have drifted, these three chapters speak to that situation with unusual directness.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well for personal reading. The book of Jude is short — just 25 verses — but dense. Working through it a section at a time, writing your answers before moving on, will help you slow down enough to hear what Jude is actually saying. The reflection questions at the end of each chapter are worth sitting with honestly.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: Jude is an excellent study for a small group, partly because it raises questions that can generate real discussion — about how to respond to false teaching, what it looks like to contend for the faith without being harsh, and how God’s character grounds our confidence. The most productive groups are usually those where each person has worked through the questions beforehand, so that discussion starts from real engagement rather than cold reading.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone walking through this study with you — a pastor, a more experienced Christian, or a trusted friend who can help you think through some of the harder material. Jude covers topics that can feel abstract or even unsettling: judgment, apostasy, the danger of error within the church. A good leader helps ground those themes in real life.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This lets them see what you have engaged with before you meet, and makes the conversation much richer.

blocks.title: Three kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter begins with a short set of observation questions — straightforward questions about what the text actually says, designed to make sure you have read it carefully before moving on. These are followed by Bible questions tied to specific passages, asking you to think about what the text means. Finally, reflection questions at the end ask what the chapter means for you personally.

blocks.title: The observation questions matter
blocks.body: It can be tempting to skip past the opening questions because they seem too simple. Don’t. Jude is a carefully written letter, and the details matter. Noticing exactly who Jude addresses, what specific words he uses, and what examples he chooses is the foundation for understanding everything else. Slow down and look.

leadersNotesData.subtitle: Truth Unlocked
intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, thoughtful mentor for a Bible study series called ‘Jude for Today’ — exploring the New Testament book of Jude.
